learningGoal: How to find the perimeter by adding all the sides
Why 2 x (length + width) is a shortcut for rectangles

screens.title: How much fencing/ribbon does Mr Singh need?
screens.body:
Mr Singh is putting a fence around the school vegetable patch. The rectangle is **12m** long and **8m** wide.
The **perimeter (the total distance around the outside)** — let's find it!

screens.title: Add all four sides!
screens.body: A rectangle has **4 sides**. To find the perimeter, add them all up. Follow the steps below!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Add the sides step by step to get 40m. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by adding the two lengths, then the two widths, then combine them.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A rectangle is **11m** long and **7m** wide. What is the perimeter?

interaction.question: What is the perimeter?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The perimeter is **36m**. You added all four sides! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Perimeter = 11 + 7 + 11 + 7 = **36m**. Remember to add ALL four sides!

screens.title: The perimeter recipe!
screens.body: **Perimeter** = the total distance around the outside. Here's how to find it:

steps.why: Add length and width, then double it.
All four sides are the same!
Perimeter = adding sides. Area = multiplying sides. ✓

learningGoal: How to spot the common mistake of forgetting to double
Why length + width is only HALF the perimeter

screens.title: Can you spot Harry's mistake?
screens.body:
Harry is calculating how much tape he needs to go around a parcel. The rectangle is **14cm** by **9cm**.
Harry says the perimeter (the total distance around the outside) is **23cm**. That's not right!

screens.title: Don't forget to DOUBLE!
screens.body: Harry only added length + width once (14 + 9 = 23) instead of doubling it. The **perimeter** (total distance around the outside) needs all **4 sides** — two lengths and two widths. Tap to see the wrong way and the right way side by side.

screens.title: What's the correct perimeter?
screens.body: A rectangle is **13cm** long and **8cm** wide. What is the perimeter?

interaction.question: What is the perimeter?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **2 x (13 + 8) = 42cm**. You remembered to double! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Perimeter = 2 × (13 + 8) = **42cm**. Make sure you count all four sides!

screens.title: Remember: all FOUR sides!
screens.body: The number one perimeter mistake is adding only **two** sides. A rectangle has **four**!

steps.why: Double it to get all four sides.

steps.text: Quick check: is your answer roughly double (l + w)?
steps.why: If it's about the same as l + w, you forgot to double! ✓

subConcepts.name: Finding Missing Side from Known Perimeter

learningGoal: How to work backwards from the perimeter to find a missing side
Why halving the perimeter first makes it easier

screens.title: Can you find the missing side?
screens.body:
Jasmine has a rectangular garden with a perimeter of 36 m. The length is 12 m.
What is the width? You know the perimeter (the total distance around the outside) — can you work **backwards** to find the missing side?

screens.title: Work backwards in two steps!
screens.body: The perimeter is **36m** and the length is **12m**. Since perimeter counts each side **twice**, we can work backwards to find the width. Are these statements true or false?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Perimeter = **40m**. The length is **11m**. What is the width?

interaction.question: What is the width?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Halve the perimeter: 40 ÷ 2 = 20. Then subtract: 20 - 11 = **9m**.

screens.title: Finding a missing side from perimeter!
screens.body: When you know the perimeter and one side, work backwards:

steps.why: What's left is the missing side!
Plug both sides back in to verify. ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid forgetting to halve the perimeter first
Why subtracting one side from the full perimeter gives the wrong answer

screens.title: Can you spot Oscar's mistake?
screens.body:
Oscar is finding the width of a rectangular field with perimeter 50 m and length 17 m.
Oscar says the missing side is **33m**. That seems way too big!

screens.title: You must halve the perimeter first!
screens.body: Oscar subtracted from the full perimeter (50 - 17 = 33) instead of halving first. The perimeter (the total distance around the outside) includes **two** of each side, so you must halve it before subtracting. Tap to see the wrong and right approach.

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body: Perimeter = **58m**. One side = **18m**. What is the missing side?

interaction.question: What is the missing side?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Halve first: 58 ÷ 2 = 29. Then 29 - 18 = **11m**.

screens.title: Always halve the perimeter first!
screens.body: The perimeter counts each side **twice**. So to find a missing side:

steps.why: What's left is the missing side.

steps.text: If your answer is bigger than half the perimeter, something's wrong!
steps.why: A side can't be bigger than l + w combined. ✓

subConcepts.name: Finding Missing Side from Known Area

learningGoal: How to find a missing side when you know the area
Why dividing the area by the known side gives the missing side

screens.title: Can you find the missing side?
screens.body:
Mrs Collins has a rectangular flower bed with an area of 48 m². The length is 8 m.
You know **Area = length x width**. If you know the area and one side, can you work out the other side? Of course — just **divide**!

screens.title: Divide the area by the known side!
screens.body: Division undoes multiplication — so if you know the area and one side, you can find the missing side. Put the steps in order!

steps.why: We know the area and one side
Rearrange: divide area by the known side

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Start with the rule, plug in what you know, then divide. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start with the area rule, then plug in the numbers, then divide.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Area = **56m²**. The length is **7m**. What is the width?

interaction.question: What is 56 ÷ 7?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **56 ÷ 7 = 8m**. Check: 7 × 8 = 56 ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 56 ÷ 7 = **8m**. Divide the area by the side you know!

screens.title: Finding a missing side from area!
screens.body: When you know the area and one side of a rectangle:

steps.why: The answer should equal the area.
This is the same as area ÷ known = ? ✓

learningGoal: How to use division to find a missing side from the area
Why a bigger area with the same width means a longer length

screens.title: How long is it?
screens.body:
A rectangular rug covers 54 m² of floor. It is 6 m wide.
How long is it? You know the area and one side. Think about it: if 6 rows of **something** make 54, what's the **something**?

screens.title: Area ÷ one side = the other side!
screens.body: When you know the area and one side, the other side is hiding inside a division. Tap to uncover it step by step!

steps.why: Divide area by the known side

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Area = **42m²**. One side = **7m**. What is the other side?

interaction.question: What is 42 ÷ 7?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **42 ÷ 7 = 6m**. The missing side is 6m! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 42 ÷ 7 = **6m**. Think: 7 × what = 42?

screens.title: Area and division — best friends!
screens.body: Finding a missing side from area is just **division in disguise**:

steps.why: Known × missing should equal the area. ✓

subConcepts.name: Area of Triangles (half base times height)

learningGoal: How to see that a triangle is half of a rectangle
Why the rule is half x base x height

screens.title: What do you notice?
screens.body:
Imagine a rectangle that is **10cm** wide and **6cm** tall. Now draw a diagonal line corner to corner. What do you get? **Two triangles!**

Each triangle is exactly **half** the rectangle. So if the rectangle's area is 60cm², the triangle's area is...

screens.title: Triangle area = half the rectangle!
screens.body: A triangle is exactly **half** of the rectangle it fits inside. The height must be **perpendicular** (at a right angle) to the base, not slanted. Match each shape to its area rule!

steps.why: A triangle is half of the rectangle

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A triangle has a base of **8cm** and a height of **4cm**. What is the area?

interaction.question: What is ½ × 8 × 4?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! ½ × 8 × 4 = **16cm²**. You remembered to halve! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 8 × 4 = 32. Then halve it: 32 ÷ 2 = **16cm²**. Don't forget the ÷ 2!

screens.title: Triangle area — halve the rectangle!
screens.body: A triangle is **half a rectangle** with the same base and height:

steps.text: Area of triangle = ½ × base × height
Use the perpendicular height (straight up, at 90° to the base)
steps.why: Not the slanted side — the straight-up height!

steps.text: The most common mistake: forgetting to halve!
steps.why: If your answer is double what you expected, divide by 2. ✓

learningGoal: How to use the rule ½ × base × height step by step
Why you must use the perpendicular height (straight up at 90°), not the slant

screens.title: What's the area of this triangle?
screens.body:
A triangular garden bed has a base of 14 m and a height of 8 m.
Let's use the rule step by step!

screens.title: Follow the rule!
screens.body: Let's find the area of this triangle with base **14m** and height **8m**. Tap each step to see how the calculation works:

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Base = **12m**, height = **6m**. What's the area?

interaction.question: What is ½ × 12 × 6?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 12 × 6 = 72, then ÷ 2 = **36m²**. Multiply first, then halve!

screens.title: Two steps for triangle area!
screens.body: Triangle area in two easy steps:

steps.why: This gives you the rectangle area.
The triangle is half the rectangle. Done! ✓

learningGoal: How to see that a parallelogram (a slanted rectangle with two pairs of parallel sides) can be rearranged into a rectangle
Why the area rule is the same as a rectangle: base x height

screens.title: A shape-shifting trick!
screens.body:
A parallelogram looks like a **squashed rectangle**. But here's a magic trick: chop off one end and slide it to the other side... and you get a **perfect rectangle**!

That means the area rule is the same: **base × height**!

screens.title: Same as a rectangle!
screens.body: A parallelogram can be rearranged into a rectangle — so the area rule is the same! But watch out for the **slant side** trap. Fill in the missing word:

steps.why: Use the height at 90° to the base — same rule as a rectangle!

interaction.sentence: The area of a parallelogram is base × ____ height
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Use the perpendicular height (straight up at 90°), not the slant. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — you must use the perpendicular height (at a right angle to the base), not the slant side!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A parallelogram has a base of **9m** and a perpendicular height of **5m**. What is the area?

interaction.question: What is 9 × 5?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **9 × 5 = 45m²**. Same as a rectangle — just use the perpendicular height! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 9 × 5 = **45m²**. Remember: use the perpendicular height, not the slant!

screens.title: Parallelogram = rearranged rectangle!
screens.body: A parallelogram and a rectangle with the same base and height have the **same area**:

steps.why: Cut off one end, slide it across — it's a rectangle!

steps.text: Use the perpendicular height (straight up at 90°)
steps.why: NOT the slant side — that's a common trap!

learningGoal: How to apply base x height for parallelograms (slanted rectangles)
How to identify the perpendicular height (the straight-up height, at a right angle) vs the slant height

screens.title: Which height do you use?
screens.body:
A parallelogram-shaped patio is 9 m along the base and 4 m in perpendicular height. The slant side is 5 m.
Careful! There are two measurements that look like "height" — the perpendicular height of **4m** and the **slant** of 5m. Which one goes in the rule?

steps.why: The tilted edge — NOT the height!

screens.title: Always use the perpendicular height!
screens.body: The question gives you two measurements that look like heights — but only the **perpendicular** one goes in the rule. Match each measurement to what it is:

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Base = **11m**, perpendicular height = **3m**. What is the area?

interaction.question: What is the area?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **11 × 3 = 33m²**. You used the right height! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Area = base × perpendicular height = 11 × 3 = **33m²**. Did you accidentally use the slant?

screens.title: Perpendicular height — the key!
screens.body: The most common mistake with parallelograms is using the **wrong height**:

steps.why: This is what you multiply by the base.
This is NOT used in the area rule!
It shows you which measurement is the perpendicular height. ✓

learningGoal: How to split an L-shape into two rectangles
How to find the area of each rectangle and add them

screens.title: An L-shape — how do you find the area?
screens.body:
Mrs Walters is tiling an L-shaped kitchen floor. The shape isn't a simple rectangle — it's an **L-shape**!
The trick: **split it into two rectangles**, find each area, then **add** them together.

screens.title: Split, calculate, add!
screens.body: Draw a line to split the L-shape into **Rectangle A** and **Rectangle B**. Tap to reveal the steps!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Rectangle A is **9m × 5m**. Rectangle B is **4m × 3m**. What is the total area?

interaction.question: What is 45 + 12?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **45 + 12 = 57m²**. Split, calculate, add — you've got it! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 45 + 12 = **57m²**. Add the two rectangle areas together!

screens.title: Compound shapes — split and add!
screens.body: Any compound shape can be split into simpler shapes:

steps.text: Step 1: Draw a line to split it into rectangles
steps.why: Look for where you can divide it neatly.

steps.text: Step 2: Find the area of each rectangle

steps.why: Total area = Area A + Area B. ✓

learningGoal: How to spot mistakes in splitting compound shapes (shapes made from simpler shapes joined together)
Why you need to use the right dimensions for each rectangle

screens.title: Can you spot Tyler's mistake?
screens.body:
Tyler is finding the area of an L-shaped room.
Tyler says the area is **135m²**. But the correct answer is much smaller!

screens.title: Split it properly!
screens.body: Tyler multiplied the total length by the total width (9 × 15 = 135) as if it were a full rectangle, instead of splitting the L-shape. You must split the L-shape into **two separate rectangles** and use the correct dimensions for each. Tap to see the right approach.

steps.why: multiplied the total length by the total width (9 × 15 = 135) as if it were a full rectangle, instead of splitting the L-shape

screens.title: What's the correct area?
screens.body: Rectangle A: **8 × 4** = 32. Rectangle B: **5 × 3** = 15. Total?

interaction.question: What is the total area?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **32 + 15 = 47m²**! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 32 + 15 = **47m²**. Make sure you're adding, not multiplying!

screens.title: Don't treat an L-shape as a rectangle!
screens.body: The biggest compound shape mistake is treating the whole shape as one rectangle:

steps.text: An L-shape is NOT a full rectangle
steps.why: A corner is missing — so you can't just do total l × total w.
Each rectangle gets its OWN length and width.
Total area = sum of the parts. ✓

subConcepts.name: Same Area Does Not Mean Same Perimeter

learningGoal: How to understand that two shapes can have the same area but different perimeters
Why area and perimeter measure different things

screens.title: Same area — same perimeter?
screens.body:
Ella is comparing two rectangular gardens.

Rectangle A is **6m × 4m**.
Rectangle B is **12m × 2m**.

Both have the same area (**24m²**). But do they have the same perimeter (the total distance around the outside)?

screens.title: Area and perimeter are DIFFERENT!
screens.body: These two rectangles have the same area — but very different perimeters! Match each concept to its meaning:

screens.title: Find the perimeter!
screens.body: A rectangle is **8m** long and **3m** wide. What is its perimeter?

steps.why: Add length and width, then double

interaction.question: What is the perimeter?
interaction.correctFeedback: Correct! 2 × (8 + 3) = **22m**. Perimeter is the distance around the outside! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 2 × (8 + 3) = **22m**. Remember to double!

screens.title: Area and perimeter — not the same!
screens.body: This is a really important thing to remember:

steps.text: Same area does NOT mean same perimeter
steps.why: A long thin shape has more perimeter than a square-ish shape with the same area.
Measured in cm, m, etc. — no ² symbol! ✓

learningGoal: How to tell area and perimeter apart in exam questions
Why the units are different (cm² vs cm)

screens.title: Area or perimeter — which one?
screens.body:
Sophie has a rectangular swimming pool that is 10 m long and 6 m wide.

**Area** = 10 × 6 = **60m²**
**Perimeter (the total distance around the outside)** = 2 × (10 + 6) = **32m**

Notice: the **units** are different! Area uses ², perimeter doesn't.

screens.title: How to tell them apart!
screens.body:
Take Sophie's rectangle (**10m** × **6m**). Area = 10 × 6 = **60m²**. Perimeter = 2 × (10 + 6) = **32m**.

Notice the different **units** and **operations**! Fill in the blank:

steps.why: How much space inside? Units have ² → m²
How far around the outside? No ² → m

interaction.sentence: "How much carpet to cover the floor?" is asking for the ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Covering a surface means area. Perimeter is the distance around the edge. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — "covering" a surface is about the space inside, which is area!

screens.title: Find the area!
screens.body: A rectangle is **9m** long and **7m** wide. How much surface does it cover?

interaction.question: What is the area?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Covering a surface is **area** = 9 × 7 = **63m²** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Area = 9 × 7 = **63m²**. "Covering" means area (multiply length × width)!

screens.title: The cheat sheet!
screens.body: Here's how to never mix up area and perimeter again:

steps.text: AREA = inside space = multiply = cm² or m²
PERIMETER = outside edge = add = cm or m
Quick check: does the unit have ²?

learningGoal: Why 1 m² = 10,000 cm² (not 100 cm²)
How to convert between square units

screens.title: 1 m² = how many cm²?
screens.body:
Most people say **100 cm²** — but that's WRONG!

1 metre = 100 cm. But 1 **square** metre is a square that's 100 cm by 100 cm.
**100 × 100 = 10,000**

So **1 m² = 10,000 cm²**. That's a lot bigger than you'd think!

screens.title: Multiply by 10,000, not 100!
screens.body: Isaac is converting the area of his bedroom floor from m² to cm². The area is **12 m²** — but how many cm² is that? Converting area units is **not** the same as converting length units!

bodyParts.content: Since 1 m² = **10,000** cm², we multiply 12 by 10,000:

steps.why: That's only for converting lengths, not areas!

interaction.sentence: To convert m² to cm², multiply by ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 1 m² = 100 × 100 = 10,000 cm². You must square the multiplier for area! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — for area you square the conversion factor: 100² = 10,000. So multiply by 10,000!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Convert **8 m²** to cm².

interaction.question: What is 8 m² in cm²?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **8 × 10,000 = 80000 cm²**. You remembered the 10,000! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 8 × 10,000 = **80000 cm²**. For area, multiply by 10,000 (not 100)!

screens.title: Square unit conversions!
screens.body: When converting **area** (square) units, the number you multiply by gets **squared** too:

steps.why: Going to a smaller unit = bigger number
Going to a bigger unit = smaller number. ✓

learningGoal: How to spot the 100 vs 10,000 mistake
Why you must square the multiplier when converting area units

screens.title: Can you spot Lucas's mistake?
screens.body:
Lucas is converting his rectangular garden (4 m²) to cm².
Lucas says: 4 m² = **400 cm²**.
That doesn't seem right...

screens.title: × 10,000, not × 100!
screens.body:
Lucas is converting his rectangular garden (4 m²) to cm². Lucas wrote 4 × 100 = **400 cm²**, but Lucas multiplied by 100 instead of 10,000.

When you convert **area** (square) units, you must **square** the multiplier too:

steps.why: That's the length conversion, not area!

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body: Convert **6 m²** to cm².

interaction.question: What is 6 m² in cm²?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 6 × 10,000 = **60000 cm²**. For area, always multiply by 10,000!

screens.title: The conversion trap!
screens.body: The exam loves to test whether you know the difference:

learningGoal: How to find the area of a border or path around a rectangle
Why you subtract inner area from outer area

screens.title: How much path is there?
screens.body:
Mrs Green is laying a stone path around a rectangular pond.
The inner rectangle is **8m × 5m**. The path is **1m** wide all the way around.
How do you find the area of **just the path** (the yellow part)? There's a clever trick!

screens.title: Outer minus inner!
screens.body:
Mrs Green is laying a stone path around a rectangular pond. The outer rectangle is **10m × 7m** and the inner one is **8m × 5m**.

To find the area of just the path, work out the **whole thing**, then **subtract** the inside. Put the steps in order!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Outer area first, then inner, then subtract. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — find the outer area first, then the inner area, then subtract to get the path.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Outer area = **63m²**. Inner area = **35m²**. What is the area of just the path?

interaction.question: What is 63 − 35?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **63 − 35 = 28m²**. The path area is 28m²! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 63 − 35 = **28m²**. Subtract the inner from the outer!

screens.title: Paths, borders, frames — same trick!
screens.body: Whenever you need the area of a border or path around a rectangle:

steps.why: The path adds width to BOTH sides — so add 2× path width to length AND width.
What's left is just the path/border! ✓

learningGoal: How to calculate the outer dimensions when a path width is given
How to subtract areas step by step

screens.title: How much border is there?
screens.body:
A rectangular swimming pool (6 m × 4 m) has a 2 m wide path around it.
The path is on **all sides** — so the outer rectangle is bigger than the inner one. Let's work through it step by step!

screens.title: Work out the outer dimensions first!
screens.body:
A rectangular swimming pool (6 m × 4 m) has a 2 m wide path around it.

The path adds **2m** on **each side**. That's **4m** extra on both the length and the width. Tap to reveal each step!

steps.why: The whole thing including the path

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Outer area = **72m²**. Inner area = **30m²**. What is the area of just the path?

interaction.question: What is the path area?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **72 − 30 = 42m²**! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 72 − 30 = **42m²**. Subtract the inner area from the outer!

screens.title: The path/border recipe!
screens.body: For any path, border, or frame problem:

steps.text: Add TWICE the path width to each dimension
steps.why: The path goes on BOTH sides (left+right, top+bottom).

steps.text: Calculate outer area and inner area
steps.why: Outer = big rectangle. Inner = original rectangle.
Subtraction gives you just the path. ✓
